---
title: "1.2 Net Returns and Costs"
subtitle: "Video lesson slides"
author: "Algorithmic Trading"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    css: ../../../../styles/reveal.css
    slide-number: true
    controls: true
    progress: true
    transition: slide
    transition-speed: fast
    center: false
    auto-stretch: true
    auto-animate: true
    auto-slide: 9000
    auto-slide-stoppable: true
    show-notes: false
    scrollable: true
    embed-resources: false
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Gross Is Not Tradable

Gross returns answer:

**Did the signal line up with future returns before practical constraints?**

Net returns answer:

**Did the edge survive implementation?**

::: {.notes}
This section is the bridge from a promising signal to a tradable strategy.
:::

## One-Period Model

$$
r^{\text{net}}_t = w_{t-1}r_t - c_t
$$

where $c_t$ includes costs paid because the portfolio changed.

::: {.notes}
The position is delayed and the cost is charged at the moment of exposure
change. Those two details eliminate many inflated tests.
:::

## Turnover Drives Costs

$$
\text{Turnover}_t = |w_t - w_{t-1}|
$$

$$
c_t = \alpha \cdot \text{Turnover}_t + \beta \cdot \sigma_t
$$

::: {.notes}
Costs are tied to trading, not to the final position alone. The simple model can
be expanded later for borrow, fees, spread, and impact.
:::

## Equity Curve Needs Context

![](../../../../assets/example-pnl.svg){fig-alt="Example cumulative PnL line chart" width="86%"}

::: {.notes}
The curve is a starting point, not the conclusion. Always ask whether costs,
capacity, and tail outcomes can explain the apparent smoothness.
:::

## Distribution Check

::: columns
::: {.column width="52%"}
![](../../../../assets/example-histogram.svg){fig-alt="Example returns histogram" width="100%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
Inspect:

- tail losses
- skew
- volatility clustering
- whether Sharpe hides rare events
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
The distribution slide prevents the audience from over-reading a smooth
cumulative PnL chart.
:::

## Review Before Trust

- Is the position shifted before returns are applied?
- Are costs applied to turnover?
- Are borrow, spread, and slippage visible assumptions?
- Does performance survive realistic capacity constraints?

::: {.notes}
Close by turning the section into a checklist. This makes the next coding or
research step clear.
:::
